items.question:
A 48-year-old man is referred to you for further treatment of acromegaly, which was diagnosed 3 years earlier when he presented with a bitemporal visual field deficit. At that time, he underwent transsphenoidal resection of a 2-cm macroadenoma, but only a partial resection could be done. Postoperatively, a residual tumor of the left side of the sella with a maximal diameter of 0.5 cm remained, but it was not near the optic chiasm.

Initial postoperative laboratory test results:
GH = 8.0 ng/mL (8.0  μg/L)
IGF-1 = 780 ng/mL (102.2 nmol/L)
Prolactin = 16 ng/mL (0.70 nmol/L)

For the last 2 years, he has been treated with monthly long-acting somatostatin analogue injections. At the maximum dosage, he has achieved a GH concentration no lower than 3.0 ng/mL (3.0 μg/L) and an IGF-1 concentration no lower than 428 ng/mL (56.1 nmol/L). The patient has had difficult-to-treat type 2 diabetes mellitus for 1.5 years and Hemoglobin A1c level of 8.0% (0.080). A recent pituitary MRI shows no change in the residual tumor from its appearance on the first postoperative scan.

One month ago, his long-acting somatostatin analogue was discontinued and pegvisomant, 10 mg daily, was begun.

Which of the following is the most likely outcome of switching to pegvisomant?
items.explanation:
15 ANSWER: E) Improvement in diabetes mellitus

This patient has persistent acromegaly that was partially resistant to somatostatin analogue therapy after transsphenoidal surgery. Therefore, additional therapy is indicated and pegvisomant is a reasonable next therapeutic option for him. Given that this patient has diabetes, pegvisomant is especially appropriate medical therapy. Although the lowering of GH levels with somatostatin analogue therapy should improve insulin resistance in patients with acromegaly, these medications also suppress insulin levels. Thus, glucose intolerance and diabetes may not improve with somatostatin analogues. Pegvisomant, however, has been shown to improve insulin sensitivity and lower Hemoglobin A1c levels in patients with acromegaly (thus, Answer E is correct).

Pegvisomant therapy is begun at a dosage of 10 mg daily and is titrated to achieve a normal IGF-1 concentration. In a small percentage of patients (2%-3%), liver enzymes rise on pegvisomant therapy. This tends to occur in the first 6 months of therapy and is usually transient. It is recommended that liver enzymes be monitored monthly after pegvisomant is initiated. If they rise to more than 3 times the upper normal limit of the reference range, pegvisomant therapy should be held. Therefore, monitoring liver enzymes regularly, especially in the first 6 months is necessary, but the development of abnormalities is unlikely (thus, Answer A is incorrect). If pegvisomant is held because of elevated liver enzymes, a re-challenge can be done if the liver enzymes normalize off therapy. Rarely do persistent enzyme elevations limit the use of pegvisomant.

Pituitary tumor size must also be monitored during pegvisomant therapy. However, the risk of pituitary tumor enlargement is small, roughly 3% over a 5-year period (thus, Answer B is incorrect). Pegvisomant does not

accelerate pituitary tumor growth. In patients taking pegvisomant, GH-secreting tumors seem to enlarge at the previous growth rate. Patients at higher risk for tumor enlargement are those whose tumors have been shown to enlarging before therapy, those who had significant shrinkage on somatostatin analogue therapy that is withdrawal or those who have not had radiotherapy. Although not necessary in this patient, if radiotherapy had been performed it would be expected to have reduced the chance of tumor enlargement on pegvisomant.

Tachyphylaxis to pegvisomant is not reported (thus, Answer D is incorrect).

Pegvisomant does not act on pituitary tumor GH secretion, but rather treats acromegaly by blocking peripheral receptors, thus lowering IGF-1 levels. On pegvisomant, GH levels remain the same or rise somewhat. Therefore, only IGF-1 levels, not GH levels, are monitored as a marker of pegvisomant's effectiveness (thus, Answer C is incorrect).

Educational Objective:

Treat acromegaly with pegvisomant and discuss the benefits and risks of this therapy.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of acromegaly

Reference(s):

Trainer PJ, Ezzat S, D'Souza GA, Layton G, Strasburger CJ. A randomized, controlled, multicentre trial comparing pegvisomant alone with combination of pegvisomant and long-acting octreotide in patients with acromegaly. Clin Endocrinol (Oxf). 2009;71(4):549-557.

Buchfelder M, Weigel D, Droste M, et al; Investigators of German Pegvisomant Observational Study. Pituitary tumor size in acromegaly during pegvisomant treatment: experience from MR re-evaluations of the German Pegvisomant Observational Study. Eur J Endocrinol. 2009;161(1):27-35.

items.question:
A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and vision problems, both of which have progressively worsened over the last 6 months. Head CT reveals a large sellar mass that extends suprasellarly and distorts the optic chiasm. The mass extends into the clivus and cavernous sinuses bilaterally. The patient has no relevant medical history and takes no medications. On further questioning, he describes noticing reduced libido and sexual dysfunction for about 1 year.
On physical examination, he appears healthy. Heart rate is 76 beats/min, and blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg. Weight is 187 lb (85 kg), and height is 71 in (180.3 cm) (BMI = 26.1 kg/m²). Findings on neurologic examination are normal, except for bitemporal hemianopsia documented on visual field examination.

Laboratory test results (blood drawn at 8 AM):
TSH = 1.0 mIU/L
Prolactin = 1280 ng/mL (55.7 nmol/L)
Free  T4 = 0.9 ng/dL (11.6 pmol/L) GH = 0.5 ng/mL (0.5  μg/L)
Cortisol = 18.0  μg/dL (497 nmol/L)

Transsphenoidal surgical removal of the lesion is scheduled for tomorrow morning. Because of the hormonal abnormalities, the neurosurgeon contacts you for an opinion regarding the patient's management.

Which of the following should you recommend next?
options: Proceed with surgery and add dexamethasone, 4 mg every 6 hours
Proceed with surgery and plan for gamma-knife radiotherapy postoperatively
Postpone surgery and begin octreotide, 50 mcg twice daily
Postpone surgery and begin bromocriptine, 2.5 mg daily
items.explanation:
24 ANSWER: E) Postpone surgery and begin bromocriptine, 2.5 mg daily

This patient presents with a macroprolactinoma and associated vision loss. The initial treatment of choice for such patients is a dopamine agonist (Answer E). Either bromocriptine as in Answer E or cabergoline, which was not given as a therapeutic option, is an appropriate choice for dopamine agonist therapy. A trial of dopamine agonist for a patient with a clear diagnosis of prolactinoma is warranted before surgery (thus, Answers A, B, and C are incorrect). Dopamine agonists can rapidly shrink the tumor and improve vision. Surgery is reserved for scenarios when patients have severe, rapid vision loss or when pituitary apoplexy is unresponsive to conservative management. Dopamine agonists normalize prolactin levels in about 80% of men with macroprolactinomas and induce clinically significant pituitary tumor shrinkage in up to 90%. Vision impairment often improves within days of initiating dopamine agonist therapy. Prolactinomas, particularly in men, can grow very large before they are recognized, such as the tumor described in this vignette. Prolactinomas often have a characteristic, very invasive appearance on MRI.

High-dosage dexamethasone (Answer B) does not shrink macroprolactinomas and there is no indication for its use in the treatment of secondary adrenal insufficiency given this patient's normal cortisol level. Octreotide (Answer D) is also not indicated because he has a low GH level and no evidence of GH hypersecretion. Octreotide can shrink GH-secreting tumors, but it rarely achieves this rapidly enough to improve vision, so initial management of GH-secreting pituitary tumors, especially those associated with vision loss, is surgery. Radiotherapy after surgery (Answer C) is reserved for patients with known recurrence not amenable to surgical removal or a hormone-secreting tumor that cannot be treated medically.

Educational Objective:

Manage a macoprolactinoma presenting with vision loss

UpToDate Topic

Reference(s): Colao A, Savastano S. Medical treatment of prolactinomas. Nat Rev Endocrinol. 2011;7(5):267-278. Melmed S, Casanueva FF, Hoffman AR, et al; Endocrine Society. Diagnosis and treatment of hyperprolactinemia: an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011;96(2):273-288.

items.question:
You are asked to see a 38-year-old woman who underwent successful transphenoidal resection of a nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma 6 months ago. Postoperative endocrine testing demonstrated anterior pituitary dysfunction, and the following medications were prescribed: hydrocortisone, 25 mg daily in divided doses; levothyroxine, 150 mcg daily; and a combined oral contraceptive pill (norethindrone and ethinyl estradiol, 1/35). She has been experiencing regular and predictable withdrawal bleeds since starting the oral contraceptive pill. Despite good adherence to her treatment regimen, she continues to experience lethargy, general malaise, and low mood.

On physical examination, she appears well. Weight is 141 lb (64.1 kg), and height is 66 in (167.6 cm) (BMI 22.8 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 124/79 mm Hg (supine) with no notable postural drop. She appears euthyroid.

Laboratory test results:
Sodium = 137 mEq/L (137 mmol/L)
Potassium = 4.4 mEq/L (4.4 mmol/L)
GH = <0.01 ng/mLquad (<0.01 μg/L)
Creatinine = 0.7 mg/dL (61.9 μmol/L)
TSH = 2.1 mIU/L
DHEA-S = 14 μg/dL (0.38 μ mol/L)

GH therapy is initiated. Which of the following adjustments to her current endocrine therapy may be required?
options: Switch from the combined oral contraceptive pill to transdermal estrogen and progestin
items.explanation:
31 ANSWER: C) Switch from the combined oral contraceptive pill to transdermal estrogen and progestin GH-mediated stimulation of hepatic IGF-1 production can be influenced by gonadal steroids; specifically, oral estrogen is a negative regulator of this process. Therefore, continuation of oral estrogen replacement in this patient could reduce the effectiveness of GH therapy or result in a requirement for a higher than necessary GH dosage to achieve satisfactory IGF-1 levels. This negative effect on IGF-1 production is less evident when estrogen is administered transdermally (thus, Answer C is correct and Answer A is incorrect).

In contrast, there is evidence that androgen replacement may stimulate IGF-1 production. Therefore, DHEA (Answer E) can be associated with a decrease in the GH dosage required to reach satisfactory IGF-1 levels. However, this is not an indication to start DHEA replacement. Indeed, the Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline on androgen therapy in women recommends against making such a diagnosis because there is no clearly defined clinical syndrome associated with androgen deficiency in women, the natural history of androgen production in healthy women is unclear, and there is no consistent benefit associated with androgen replacement in clinical trials. Finally, in the United States, DHEA is widely available in over-the-counter formulations, which has implications for quality control.

GH replacement lowers serum cortisol levels, probably due to enhanced conversion of cortisone to cortisol in the GH-deficient state, thus unmasking central hypoadrenalism. Therefore, it is important to ensure that the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis is normal before commencing GH therapy. However, if the patient is already on an adequate dosage of corticosteroid replacement (and this patient is), there is no automatic requirement to adjust glucocorticoid replacement dosages (Answer B).

Finally, it has been demonstrated that GH replacement can lower serum free  T4 levels, perhaps due to increased deiodination of  T4. Thus, free  T4 levels should be monitored during GH treatment, and the levothyroxine dosage may need to be increased rather than decreased (although this is not often necessary) (thus, Answer D is incorrect).

Educational Objective:

Assess whether adjustment of other endocrine replacement therapies is necessary in a patient with panhypopitற்றal, who is commencing growth hormone treatment.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of hypopituitarism

Reference(s):

Molitch ME, Clemmons DR, Malozowski S, Merriam GR, Vance ML; Endocrine Society. Evaluation and treatment of adult growth hormone deficiency: an Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011;96(6):1587-1609.

Porretti S, Giavoli C, Ronchi C, et al. Recombinant human GH replacement therapy and thyroid function in a large group of adult GH-deficient patients who does L-T(4) therapy become mandatory? J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2002;87(5):2042-2045.

items.question:
A 19-year-old man is referred to you for further treatment of gigantism that was diagnosed 3 months ago. His initial workup was prompted by a physical examination done for his college basketball team. During this examination, it was documented that he had grown one-half inch in the preceding 6 months and that he had noticed changes in the features of his face, hands, and feet that were suggestive of acromegaly. Laboratory test results were consistent with acromegaly (see Table). A 2-cm sellar/suprasellar pituitary macroadenoma was documented on MRI. The patient underwent transsphenoidal resection of the tumor. The surgeon considered the resection to be complete. Now, 2 months after the operation, MRI shows postoperative changes, but no visible tumor mass. Other pituitary function is normal, including a testosterone measurement. A hand x-ray shows fused epiphyses. Findings on cardiac echocardiogram are normal.
MeasurementPreoperatively1 Month Postoperatively2 Months Postoperatively
GH28 ng/mL (28 μ g/L)0.8 ng/mL (0.8 μ g/L)0.5 ng/mL (0.5 μ g/L)
IGF-11300 ng/mL (170.3 nmol/L)686 ng/mL (89.9 nmol/L)587 ng/mL (76.9 nmol/L)
Prolactin28 ng/mL (1.2 nmol/L)10 ng/mL (0.4 nmol/L)...
When you evaluate the patient today, he feels well. His mother reports thinning of his face and hands since his operation. On physical examination, height is 82.5 in (209.6 cm) and weight is 267 lb (121.4 kg) (BMI = 27.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 110/76 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 64 beats/min. He has a slightly prominent lower jaw and large, but not full, hands and feet.
items.explanation:
## 35 ANSWER: E) No additional therapy now

This young man presented with gigantism, which is defined as acromegaly due to a GH-secreting pituitary tumor that presents in a patient younger than age 18 years. Postoperatively, his IGF-1 level has been falling, although it is not yet normal 2 months after surgery. His random GH level is low, but this alone is not used to distinguish remission from disease persistence. Only a random GH level that is very low, and less than the glucose-suppressed GH cutoff expected for remission, could be used to support a remission. Given his decreasing IGF-1 level and other aspects of his surgical and postoperative course, this patient is most likely in remission. IGF-1 levels often continue to fall during the first 3 months after successful resection of GH-secreting pituitary tumors, and some patients who achieve remission do not have a normal IGF-1 level until even longer after surgery. In a patient with a decreasing IGF-1 level and a GH level suggestive of remission, continued monitoring of IGF-1 and GH should be instituted and additional treatment of acromegaly should not be started too early after surgery. IGF-1 levels fall with age after puberty, so the upper normal limit of IGF-1 will fall every year for this young man. If he is truly in remission, this will be proven with continued follow-up. In another month (3 months postoperatively), IGF-1 and glucose-suppressed GH should be measured again. His glucose-suppressed GH level obtained 3 months after surgery should be less than 0.5 ng/mL (0.5 μg/L) when measured with most commercially available GH assays. Even without certainty about his disease status now, his clinical course should be followed without intervention for the time being (thus, Answer E is correct and Answers A, B, C, and D are incorrect).

A number of pitfalls exist when interpreting GH and IGF-1 levels both for diagnosis and determining remission in patients with acromegaly. Discrepant test results, with one hormone being elevated and the other being normal, is fairly common and can make determination of disease status difficult. In the diagnostic evaluation for acromegaly or in the evaluation of postoperative remission in a patient with known acromegaly, it is important to consider other conditions that can lead to discrepant GH and IGF-1 results. IGF-1 levels are only rarely elevated in conditions other than acromegaly, but they can be high in the setting of puberty, pregnancy, or hyperthyroidism. IGF-1 levels can be low in the context of hypothyroidism, malnutrition, poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus, liver failure, renal failure, and oral estrogen use. GH can be elevated and fail to suppress normally in the absence of an acromegaly diagnosis in patients with liver disease, renal insufficiency, malnutrition, anorexia nervosa, or hyperthyroidism, as well as during adolescence or in patients taking oral estrogen.

Educational Objective: Guide postoperative management of acromegaly on the basis of the biochemical evaluation and describe the pitfalls of testing.

UpToDate Topic Review(s): Treatment of acromegaly

Reference(s):

Freda PU. Monitoring of acromegaly: what should be performed when GH and IGF-I levels are discrepant? Clin Endocrinol (Oxf). 2009;71(2):166-170. Feelders RA, Bidingmaier M, Strasburger CJ, et al. Postoperative evaluation of patients with acromegaly: clinical significance and timing of oral glucose tolerance testing and measurement of (free) insulin-like growth factor I, acid-labile subunit, and growth hormone-binding protein levels. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2005;90:6480-6489.

items.question:
A 56-year-old man is referred to you for further evaluation of Cushing syndrome. He initially presented 2 months ago with central weight gain, facial fullness, and muscle weakness that had developed over the preceding 3 to 6 months. Physical examination performed by his primary care physician demonstrated plethora, supraclavicular fullness and a dorsocervical fat pad, central obesity with purple striae, proximal muscle weakness and new-onset hypertension. Cushing syndrome was suspected. Laboratory test results: ACTH = 140 pg/mL (30.8 pmol/L) Urinary free cortisol = 210 μg/24 h (579.6 nmol/d) Potassium = 2.8 mEq/L (2.8 mmol/L) Dexamethasone suppression testing: Serum cortisol (8 AM) = 18 μg/dL (496.6 nmol/L) Serum cortisol (8 AM) after dexamethasone, 1 mg the evening before = 17 μg/dL (469.0 nmol/L) Serum cortisol (8 AM) after dexamethasone, 8 mg the evening before = 15 μg/dL (413.8 nmol/L) MRI of the pituitary and sellar region shows a 2-mm microadenoma in the right side of the pituitary gland. ## 47 ANSWER: E) Lumbar spine bone mineral density

Primary hyperparathyroidism is a common disorder. The incidence is reported to be as high as 1 in 1000 in some studies, although it may be becoming less frequent because of a decline in ionizing radiation exposure. Women are 2 to 3 times more likely than men to be affected, and the condition generally presents after age 45 years. There are additional risk factors for developing the condition including exposure to head and neck irradiation; a family history of hyperparathyroidism (there are multiple genetic syndromes in which the risk of hyperparathyroidism is increased); and the use of certain medications, most commonly lithium. Lithium has been associated with both glandular hyperplasia of parathyroid tissue, as well as a possible shift in the set point of the calcium-sensing receptor in parathyroid cells, leading to an excessive release of PTH. In patients with bipolar psychiatric disease, lithium use is relatively common. Even if the patient is not currently treated with lithium, one must query for historical use because the hyperparathyroidism can persist despite discontinuation of the medication. The therapeutic goal is to identify symptomatic patients with known complications or currently asymptomatic patients with a high likelihood of developing complications and to refer them for surgical intervention.

Current (2009) recommendations for surgery for asymptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism are outlined as follows:

1. Serum calcium concentration greater than 1.0 mg/dL (0.25 mmol/L) above the upper normal limit;

2. Creatinine clearance (calculated) reduced to less than 60 mL/min;

3. Bone mineral density T score less than -2.5 at any site and/or previous fragility fracture

4. Age younger than 50 years

The most clinically significant complications of untreated symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism are renal up to 20% of this patient population experiences kidney stones, which are usually composed of calcium oxal. Other renal complications include chronic renal insufficiency and renal tubular dysfunction. Although this par has hypercalciuria, she has no history of kidney stones. Interestingly, urinary calcium excretion does not predict the development of stones. In the absence of stones, elevated urinary calcium is not considered an indication of parathyroid surgery. A 24-hour urinary calcium collection is still nonetheless an important part of the biochem evaluation to further evaluate for disorders of the calcium-sensing receptor. Glomerular filtration rate and ren function must be closely monitored in patients with untreated primary hyperparathyroidism. A decline in ren function and glomerular filtration rate, not otherwise accounted for by other medical issues, is an indication of surgical intervention. In this patient, the creatinine excretion and estimated glomerular filtration rate are normal a change following surgical cure is unlikely (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

Cardiovascular complications of primary hyperparathyroidism include left ventricular hypertrophy and hypertension. Only a small number of patients have neurocognitive symptoms related to hypercalcemia, although some patients report depression and a decline in neurocognition and quality-of-life parameters if the hypercalcemia is left untreated. Most studies that have examined these symptoms and shown improvement after surgery are observational and limited by selection bias. However, over the past year, his joint and back pain has worsened and he now takes morphine sulphate tablets, 50 mg twice daily, and pregabalin, 75 mg daily. In addition, he has hypertension treated with ramipril, 10 mg daily, and amlodipine, 10 mg daily.
Twice daily. In addition, he has hypertension treated with h倍剂. On physical examination, height is 72.4 in (184 cm) and weight is 202.5 lb (92 kg) (BMI = 27.2 kg/m²). He has normal 20-mL testes and normal axillary and pubic hair. No gynecomastia is noted. Blood pressure is 135/8 mm Hg (supine).

Laboratory test results:
FSH = 0.5 mIU/mL (0.5 IU/L)
Testosterone (total) = 65 ng/dL (2.3 nmol/L)
LH = 0.9 mIU/mL (0.9 IU/L)

No abnormalities are observed on pituitary MRI.

61 ANSWER: A) Switch to a non-opiate-containing analgesic

This is a case of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Given the patient's age, previous fertility, and evidence of normal secondary sexual characteristics, this is not a congenital problem. Acquired secondary hypogonadism is most often due to structural disease of the pituitary gland, hyperprolactinemia, or exogenous drug use.

In this case, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism developed because of chronic opiate use. This patient was consuming 100 mg of morphine daily. When opiates are administered on a long-term basis, especially when they are administered continuously for relief of chronic pain, they often cause pronounced secondary hypogonadism. This has been most often reported in association with high-dosage methadone use, but any opiate used for more than a few weeks at a dosage in excess of the equivalent of 100 mg of oral morphine daily can be associated with secondary hypogonadism. The mechanism is not entirely clear, but is thought to be due to inhibition of hypothalamic GnRH production by morphine. Additionally, studies have shown that opiate administration can reduce activity of 5α-reductase and thus reduce peripheral testosterone production.

Although not always practical, the optimal management strategy is to attempt to restore pituitary-gonadal function by gradual withdrawal of opiates and replacement with non-opiate-containing analgesics (Answer A). Only if this strategy fails or is not practical would consideration of testosterone replacement be appropriate. It is also important to note that long-term opiate use can also impair adrenal function via inhibition of corticotropin-releasing hormone and ACTH production.

Although sildenafil (Answer B) is an effective treatment of erectile dysfunction, it is not appropriate as a first-line strategy in this case because the underlying cause should be addressed first.

Chronic opiate use can also elevate prolactin levels, but it does not cause prolactinoma. This patient's serum prolactin concentration is mildly elevated, but this is consistent with opiate use and, crucially, is not high enough that it would be expected to be associated with secondary hypogonadism. Withdrawal of opiates should lower this patient's serum prolactin. Therefore, use of dopamine agonists such as cabergoline (Answer C) is not required. Associated with erectile dysfunction, but not with

Hypertension per se and antihypertensive drugs can be associated with hypogonadism. Although adjustment of antihypertensive therapy could be considered, this should only be done after addressing any underlying causes of erectile dysfunction. Moreover, of all antihypertensive drugs, thiazide diuretics (Answer E) and  beta-adrenergic blockers (Answer D) are associated with a higher incidence of erectile dysfunction than either ACE inhibitors or calcium channel blockers and would not be appropriate substitutes for these antihypertensive drugs.

Educational Objective:

Describe the effects of long-term opiate use on pituitary function.

Six months ago, the physician noted her TSH concentration to be 0.12 mIU/L and her levothyroxine dosage was decreased to 75 mcg daily. Three months ago, her TSH concentration was 0.07 mIU/L and her dosage was decreased to 50 mcg daily. For the past 6 months, the patient reports not feeling well, increasingly “dragging” with great trouble getting out bed in the morning and generally having poor energy. Height is 65.5 in (166.4 cm), and weight is 172 lb (78
On physical examination, she appears fatigued. Height is 65.5 in (166.4 cm), and weight is 172 lb (78.2 kg) (BMI = 28.2 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 118/78 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 49 beats/min. Her skin is dry. She has minor orbital puffiness and no stare or lid lag. Visual fields are normal. Her thyroid gland is not palpable. Reflexes can not be elicited. She has no signs of Cushing syndrome or acromegaly.

Which of the following should you recommend to determine the cause of her decreasing TSH level
items.explanation:
65 ANSWER: C) Pituitary-directed MRI

65 This patient presents with secondary hypothyroidism, and pituitary-directed MRI (Answer C) should be performed. On review of her history, a 1-cm pituitary lesion consistent with an adenoma was incidentally identified on MRI that was done 3 years earlier to evaluate dizziness. No follow-up imaging had been done. Therefore, her hypothyroidism was due to hypopituitarism caused by an enlarging pituitary tumor. Her TSH level had fallen as she had been developing hypopituitarism. This patient, in fact, had a markedly enlarged pituitary lesion compared with its size documented 3 years ago and new-onset hypopituitarism with low ACTH, cortisol, LH, and FSH in addition to secondary hypothyroidism. Her free T 4 level, when eventually measured, was low.

A pituitary incidentaloma is defined as a previously unsuspected pituitary lesion that is discovered on an imaging study performed for an unrelated reason. Pituitary incidentalomas are identified on MRI in 10% to 38% of patients undergoing the test for another reason. Most pituitary incidentalomas are microincidentalomas (ie, <1.0 cm). Patients presenting with a pituitary incidentaloma should undergo a complete history and physical examination that includes evaluation for evidence of hypopituitarism and a hormone hypersecretion syndrome. At minimum, testing should include measurement of morning cortisol, free  T4, testosterone in men, prolactin, IGF-1, and GH. Some incidentalomas are clinically unrecognized hormone-secreting pituitary tumors, so screening for hormone hypersecretion is important. Screening for prolactin excess is essential, as this is often subclinical and it is the most common abnormality detected in these patients. Screening for glucocorticoid excess due to a possible corticotroph tumor may also be considered when this is suspected clinically. Although silent somatotroph-secreting tumors are rare, GH-secreting microadenomas can be cured surgically in almost all cases, so screening for a GH-secreting tumor is warranted. This evaluation should initially include measurement of GH and IGF-1. If these are elevated, further testing can be done. An initial biochemical evaluation for hypopituitarism should be undertaken at presentation (minimally, this should include measurement of morning cortisol, free  T4, and testosterone). The biochemical evaluation for hypopituitarism can be repeated 6 months after the initial testing and yearly thereafter in patients with a pituitary macroincidentaloma, although hypopituitarism typically develops when the incidentaloma increases in size. Repeated testing for hypopituitarism in patients with pituitary microincidentalomas may not be necessary if such patients' clinical picture, history, and MRI findings do not change over time.

If initial test results are normal and the patient does not meet criteria for surgery, follow-up imaging of the pituitary can be done in 6 months for macroadenomas and in 12 months for microadenomas. Some repeated proliferative nature of the incidentaloma is unknown. In patients whose incidentaloma does not change in size,

MRI can then be repeated every year for macroincidentalomas and every 1 to 2 years for microincidentalomas for the following 3 years, then gradually less frequently thereafter.

Surgical removal of the incidentaloma in this patient at first presentation 3 years earlier would not have been indicated. Indications for surgery include a visual field deficit due to the lesion, other vision abnormalities such as ophthalmoplegia or neurologic compromise due to compression by the lesion, the lesion abutting or compressing the optic nerves or chiasm on MRI, or pituitary apoplexy with vision disturbance. If this patient had had the recommended follow-up imaging and pituitary function testing, the tumor most likely would have demonstrated the development and hypopituitarism would have been detected earlier.

A 53-year-old man is referred to you for treatment of hyperthyroidism. The patient was previously healthy, but presented with a 1-year history of anxiety, tremor, heat intolerance, and weight loss of 8 lb (3.6 kg), which led to the diagnosis.
On physical examination, weight is 175 lb (79.5 kg) and height is 72 in (182.9 cm) (BMI = 23.7 kg/m²). Heart rate is 96 beats/min. He has a small goiter and no proptosis, brisk deep tendon reflexes, and a fine tremor of his outstretched fingers.

Laboratory test results:
TSH = 3.6 mIU/L
Molar ratio of  alpha-subunit of glycoprotein hormones to intact TSH =>1
Free  T4 = 2.3 ng/dL (29.6 pmol/L)
Iodine 123 uptake = 44% at 24 hours in a homogenous pattern
Total  T3 = 294 ng/dL (4.5 nmol/L)

Pituitary MRI reveals a 2-cm macroadenoma. He undergoes transsphenoidal surgery, and the surgeon reports total resection of the tumor.

Three months postoperatively, the patient comes to see you regarding the need for further therapy.

Laboratory studies reveal persistent hyperthyroidism:
TSH = 1.86 mIU/L
Free  T4 = 3.0 ng/dL (38.6 pmol/L)
Total  T3 = 294 ng/dL (4.5 nmol/L)

MRI shows tissue on the left side of sella that may be postoperative change, but residual tumor cannot be excluded. On physical examination, his pulse rate is 98 beats/min and regular. He has a mild stare, but no proptosis. His thyroid gland is mildly enlarged, smooth, and symmetric, without palpable nodules. His skin is sweaty. He has a fine tremor of his outstretched fingers and brisk brachial reflexes. Visual fields are normal.

Which of the following is the optimal treatment to immediately control hyperthyroidism in this patient?
options: Gamma-knife radiotherapy of the residual pituitary tumor
items.explanation:
88 ANSWER: D) Octreotide TSH-secreting tumors are

TSH-secreting tumors are rare, accounting for less than 1% of pituitary adenomas. Results of preoperative laboratory testing and pathologic examination of this patient's tumor (results not included in the vignette) confirmed a TSH-secreting tumor. There is no reason to suspect a primary thyroid etiology of the hyperthyroidism or direct therapy at the thyroid itself unless efforts to control the tumor are unsuccessful.

Surgical treatment, typically by a transsphenoidal route, is the initial treatment of choice for TSH-secreting tumors. In patients not cured by surgery, treatment with a depot somatostatin analogue, such as octreotide (Answer D) is usually effective in controlling TSH hypersecretion and thus thyroid hormone levels. Octreotide can control hyperthyroidism and its symptoms by inhibiting TSH secretion from the tumor. Somatostatin analogues are not only

effective inhibitors of GH secretion, but also of TSH secretion. In one series of tumors, 21 of 25 patients treated with octreotide experienced normalized thyroid function for 61 months (the length of the study). Lanreotide also effectively reduces TSH secretion and shrinks tumors in one-third of patients.

In the past, secondary hyperthyroidism was often treated with radioactive iodine (Answer B) or antithyroid drugs (Answer A). Although these treatments can reduce  T4 levels and signs and symptoms of hyperthyroidism, thyrotoxicosis returns in most patients. These treatments inhibit the negative feedback of  T3 on TSH and can increase TSH production from the tumor. Antithyroid drugs might quickly lower thyroid levels, but therapy directed at the tumor is more desirable since somatostatin analogues can be used as very long-term therapy for TSH-secreting tumors. Saturated solution of potassium iodide (Answer E) would only control hyperthyroidism temporarily.

This patient has symptoms of hyperthyroidism, so a therapy directed at control soon is indicated. Gamma-knife radiotherapy (Answer C) could be considered for long-term disease control if somatostatin analogues are ineffective or not desired, but it would not be appropriate treatment to control hyperthyroidism now.

Educational Objective:

Summarize the recommended medical treatment options for TSH-secreting pituitary tumors.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Thyrotropin (TSH)-secreting pituitary adenomas

Reference(s):

Beck-Peccoz P, Persani L, Mannavola D, Campi I. Pituitary tumours: TSH-secreting adenomas. Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;23(5):597-606.

Before the introduction of CT, bilateral adrenal hemorrhage (as seen in the vignette's image) was often fatal and identified only at postmortem examination. Adrenal hemorrhage was initially identified in association with fulminant meningococccemia in children (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome), but this is a rare event now. Adrenal hemorrhage is usually seen in patients with a history of thromboembolic disease or recent surgery requiring either therapeutic or prophylactic anticoagulation. Coagulopathies such as heparin-induced thrombocytopenia and antiphospholipid syndrome associated with lupus anticoagulant are also well-appreciated causes of bilateral adrenal hemorrhage. The symptoms are often not dramatic with pain of varying severity in the abdomen, flank, lower back, or chest. There are usually few clues on physical examination except for a low-grade fever. Interestingly, hypotension is a late finding and usually heralds the onset of catastrophic shock that can be mistaken for septic shock.

On physical examination, height is 63 in (160 cm) and weight is 159 lb (72.3 kg) (BMI = 28.2 kg/m²). Heart rate is 72 beats/min, visual fields are normal to confrontation, and she has no galactorrhea. Laboratory test results: Serum prolactin = 34 ng/mL (1.5 nmol/L) Free T4 = 0.9 ng/dL (11.6 pmol/L) TSH = 1.0 mIU/L Cortisol = 12.0 μg/dL (331.1 nmol/L) Which of the following should be the next step?
options: Perform MRI in 6 months to evaluate for progression of the abnormality
Reassure the patient; no further evaluation is necessary
items.explanation:
105 ANSWER: E) Reassure the patient; no further evaluation is necessary

This patient's MRI demonstrates an empty sella. Empty sella is a radiographic diagnosis in which the subarachnoid space extends within the sella, such that cerebrospinal fluid is seen within the sella and the pituitary tissue is often flattened. It is a radiologic description and not a clinical diagnosis. In many cases, the empty sella appearance develops after known pituitary disease has enlarged the sella and/or altered the size and position of the pituitary gland. This is termed secondary empty sella and in adults it has been seen in association with a pituitary adenoma (including spontaneous infarction or surgical, medical, or radiation therapy for the adenoma). In addition, other pathologic processes involving the sellar region, such as Sheehan syndrome, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, spontaneous enlargement and rupture of a cyst, or granulomatous diseases affecting the pituitary, have been associated with empty sella. Empty sella not associated with one of the listed etiologies is termed primary empty sella. In adults, primary empty sella has been associated with congenital deficiency of the sellar diaphragm and benign intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri). Other risk factors for the development of an empty sella are pregnancy, obesity, and a previous autoimmune process involving the pituitary gland. Some patients with what appears to be primary empty sella may have had a pituitary disease that went undiagnosed.

Finding an empty sella on MRI is common and occurs more often in female patients. Some have suggested a relationship to previous pregnancies, with the enlarging and then shrinking of the pituitary in the postpartum period predisposing to the herniation of the sellar diaphragm into the sella and cerebrospinal fluid.

Headaches and a variety of vision disturbances have been reported in about one-third of patients with empty sella. Although previously considered very unlikely, hypopituitarism has more recently been reported in up to 40% to 50% of patients with empty sella. Hyperprolactinemia seems to be the most commonly associated endocrine abnormality. Patients with an empty sella observed on imaging should have basic testing of pituitary function, including measurement of prolactin, thyroid hormone, and morning cortisol. The chance of an endocrine abnormality is less likely in patients without clinical signs or symptoms in whom the empty sella was noted incidentally on a scan done for another reason. The need for any additional testing for hypopituitarism should be directed on the basis of the history and examination findings. Therefore, the chance of hypopituitarism in this patient is low and she should be reassured; no further evaluation is indicated (Answer E). More testing might be necessary in a different clinical scenario if a patient with empty sella had a history of a pituitary tumor or other sella lesion that may have secondarily led to the empty sella.

Given that the patient in this vignette has no clinical evidence of hormone hypersecretion and no visible tumor on MRI, there is no indication to assess her for a hormone-secreting tumor. Because she is 54 years old, whether or not her gonadotropins are elevated is not clinically relevant (thus, Answer A is incorrect). There is no evidence that incidentally noted empty sella on MRI progresses over time. Therefore, following this radiographic abnormality with serial imaging studies (Answer B) is unnecessary. This patient has hyperprolactinemia, which could be associated with the empty sella, but also could be due to paroxetine therapy. There is no need to evaluate her further for a prolactinoma because no lesion is visible. It is unlikely that the hook effect is occurring, and measuring her prolactin concentration with serial dilutions (Answer C) is incorrect. Visual field testing (Answer D) is not clinically indicated because there is no mass in her sella.

Educa

items.question:
A 58-year-old woman sees her oncologist to address a 6-month history of daily headache. Her medicine history includes treatment of estrogen-receptor positive breast cancer 4 years ago. She had clear surgical margins and negative nodes at the time of surgery. She was treated with both radiation and chemotherapy. Anastrozole, 1 mg daily, was prescribed, and she still takes this medication. There has been no evidence of recurrence and mammography of the cancer. Recurrence, and mammography of the contralateral breast has been normal.
In the past several months, she has begun to experience vague, general headaches that occur at random times. She has no history of headaches, and because they have become more frequent, she has sought advice from her oncologist. She has no evidence of polyuria, polydipsia, or nocturia. She has no history of changes in her ring or shoe size. Physical examination findings are unremarkable. There is no evidence of altered visual fields, hyperpigmentation, skin tags, striae, ecchymosis, galactorrhea. Laboratory evaluation reveals a normal chemistry panel, including a normal blood glucose concentration (fasting) and complete blood cell count. The oncologist orders brain MRI (see image) Because of the observed abnormality (arrow), she sent to you for further evaluation. You confirm the patient's history, review of systems, and physical examination findings. Dysfunction of which of the following pituitary hormones would be most likely to develop in the near term?
items.explanation:
120 ANSWER: B) Antidiuretic hormone This patient's pituitary MRI demonstrates

This patient's pituitary MRI demonstrates marked thickening of the pituitary stalk. This is an imaging finding that can be seen in diabetes insipidus due to antidiuretic hormone deficiency (Answer B). Pituitary MRI findings are variable in the context of diabetes insipidus and may be normal or demonstrate an absent posterior pituitary, loss of the posterior pituitary bright spot, or thickening of the pituitary stalk. The latter two features are more often associated with inflammatory disease such as lymphocytic hypophysitis, but they can be found in malignant (metastatic) disease. In a review of 42 adolescents presenting with pituitary stalk thickening on MRI, 16 (38%) also had diabetes insipidus. One-half of those with diabetes insipidus had neoplastic disease, whereas none of the patients with only pituitary stalk thickening (and no diabetes insipidus) had neoplastic disease. In this patient, the pituitary abnormality was due to metastatic disease from her previous breast cancer, and pituitary imaging 6 months later demonstrated a clear seller and suprasellar mass that was confirmed to be metastatic disease.

The pituitary gland is an uncommon site for metastases, as metastatic disease accounts for only 1% to 2% of sellar masses. Although neoplasms from almost every tissue have been reported to metastasize to the pituitary, the

most common primary tumors are breast or lung cancers. Metastatic deposits demonstrate a predilection for the posterior lobe of the pituitary, mainly because there is a direct arterial blood supply (via the inferior hypophyseal artery, which branches from the internal carotid artery) that facilitates hematogenous spread. In a review of more than 200 cases, the posterior lobe was involved alone or with the anterior pituitary in 85% of cases, whereas only the anterior lobe was affected in 15%. Therefore, metastatic deposits in the anterior lobe are usually due to contiguous spread from the posterior lobe.

The patient's imaging findings and previous history of breast cancer make diabetes insipidus the most likely initial endocrine dysfunction in this case. Therefore, all other answers (A, C, D, and E) are incorrect, although they may also develop with time as the metastatic disease progresses and the lesion enlarges. Indeed, in the review of 42 adolescents with pituitary stalk thickening and diabetes insipidus, 44% subsequently developed anterior pituitary dysfunction. In this case, pituitary MRI performed 6 months later demonstrated a clear sellar and suprasellar mass and the patient developed ACTH and TSH deficiencies.

Pituitary metastases are, however, often asymptomatic. In the aforementioned review, 40 of 200 patients (20%) were symptomatic, and, of these, diabetes insipidus was documented in 28 patients (70%) while only 6 patients (15%) had 1 or more anterior pituitary deficiencies.

Educational Objective:

Identify the most likely endocrine abnormality associated with pituitary neoplastic disease.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Causes, presentation, and evaluation of seller masses

Reference(s):

Robison NJ, Prabhu SP, Sun P, et al. Predictors of neoplastic disease in children with isolated pituitary stalk thickening. Pediatr Blood Cancer. 2013;60(10):1630-1635.

Komninos J, Vlassopoulou V, Protopapa D, et al. Tumors metastatic to the pituitary gland: case report and literature review. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2004;89(2):574-580.
